content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Judith Propp 
Acting Town Attorney 
350 Sansome Street, Suite 300 
San Francisco, CA 94104 
J propp@publiclaWb'fOUp.com 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-124 
Dear Ms. Propp: 
May 23, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Corte Madera Councilmcmber 
James Andrews regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the 
"Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. In addition, note that we are also not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (/n re Oglesby ( 1975) l FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are 
complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
May Coundlmcmber James Andrews participate in governmental decisions related to a 
proposed land use project when a party appearing before the Town Council at a public hearing is 
represented by an attorney who previously represented Councilmembcr Andrews? 
CONCLUSION 
Assuming the refund of money did not include money rightfully owed to Mr. Yates for 
service provided and only consisted of funds belonging to the Councilmember, Mr. Yates would not 
be a financial interest (source of income) to the Councilmember. 
1 The Political Reform Act 1s con tamed in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Go\'emment Code. unless otherwise md1cated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code ofRegulauons. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
Prior to Being Elected to the Council 
File No. A-17-124 
Page No. 2 
In 2015, before being elected to the Town Council, Councilmember Andrews as a resident 
of the Town of Corte Madera and private citizen (not an elected or appointed official at that time), 
retained the services of Attorney Edward Yates, a local land use attorney who has an office in 
nearby Larkspur. 
Mr. Yates was hired to provide legal advice on a land use project being considered by the 
Town of Corte Madera in 2015. Mr. Andrews requested Mr. Yates to prepare a public comment 
letter which was sent to the Town of Corte Madera for a hearing before the Planning Commission 
on an approximate 46-unit townhomc project at 1441 Casa Buena Drive, Corte Madera. 
Councilmember Andrews paid Mr. Yates a retainer in advance of the work and was charged 
the customary and usual hourly rate. Councilmember Andrews made his last payment to Mr. Yates 
in October 2015. Mr. Yates concluded his legal services to Mr. Andrews in late 2015. 
Election to the Town Co1111cil 
Councilmember Andrews was elected to the Corte Madera Town Council in November 
2015 and took the oath of office in December 2015. 
Mr. Yates is now representing the owners of a single-family residence located at 159 Prince 
Royal Drive, Corte Madera, CA and, the owners have a pending land use application for design 
review permit approval (Project) before the Town of Corte Madera. The property is located within 
the Town limits of Corte Madera. Attorney Yates' clients who reside at 159 Prince Royal Drive 
obtained an approval of their design review permit application by the Town of Corte Madera 
Planning Commission. 
A neighbor filed an appeal and the land use application/appeal is now pending before the 
Corte Madera Town Council. The appeal hearing for 159 Prince Royal Drive was commenced by 
the Town Council on May 2, 2017. 
Councilmember Andrews contacted the FPPC advice line for informal advice prior to the 
hearing. Councilmember Andrews recused himself from participating in this hearing since the 
applicants are being represented by Mr. Yates. He disclosed that he had previously been represented 
by Mr. Yates and was not participating in the hearing on this Project until he could obtain advice 
from the FPPC. 
The hearing for 159 Prince Royal Drive was not concluded and is set for further hearing 
before the Corte Madera Town Council on May 16, 2017. In the meantime, on April 30, 2017, 
Councilmember Andrews formally notified Mr. Yates that their dormant attorney-client relationship 
was terminated. Mr. Yates agreed to refund any unused retainer amounts to Councilmember 
Andrews which is estimated to be approximately S l, 700.00. 


On May 19, 2017, you provided the following additional infonnation: 
FileNo.A-17-124 
Page No. 3 
• The Councilmember paid Mr. Yates retainers in advance of work (i.e., drafting the comment 
letters). Mr. Yates then charged hours worked against the retainers. 
• Mr. Yates also provided the Councilmember with informal legal advice and answers when 
he had questions about land use issues. After the Councilmember was elected, he stopped 
relying on Mr. Yates for legal advice, since he then had access to the Town attorney, and 
instructed Mr. Yates to keep any unspent retainer balance as compensation for any unbilled, 
informal advice he had provided. 
• 
Despite the Councilmember instructing Mr. Yates to treat the hours spent discussing land 
use issues with the Councilmember as billable hours and to charge these billable hours to the 
unspent retainer balance, it appears that Mr. Yates did not always charge for his time. The 
Councilmember did not ask for a reconciliation of the unspent retainer amounts and 
incorrectly assumed that the outstanding retainer balance was only a couple of hundred 
dollars. 
• After the Councilmember notified Mr. Yates on April 30, 2017 that the Councilmembcr was 
formally terminating the dormant attorney-client relationship, Mr. Yates said that he would 
refund the balance of the retainer account. The Councilmembcr was surprised when 
Mr. Yates notified him that the balance was Sl,700. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making. participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions only apply to conflicts of 
interests based on the following: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth S2,000 
or more. ( Section 87103( b ). ) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on tenns available to the public without regard to official status. 
aggregating S500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 


File No. A-17-124 
Page No. 4 
• 
Any donor of, or any intennediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $4 70 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87 l03(e).) 
• 
In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or 
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
Sources of Income 
"lncome" is defined in Section 82030(a) as: 
"'Income' means, except as provided in subdivision (b), a payment received, 
including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, 
proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food or beverage, loan, 
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer, reimbursement for 
expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by 
any person other than an employer, and including any community property 
interest in the income of a spouse. Income also includes an outstanding loan. 
Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly 
or beneficially, a JO-percent interest or greater. "Income," other than a gift, docs 
not include income received from any source outside the jurisdiction and not 
doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the 
jurisdiction, or not having done business within the jurisdiction during the two 
years prior to the time any statement or other action is required under this title." 
The Act, in Section 82044, defines a "payment" as: 
"'Payment' means a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or 
other rendering of money. property, ser\'ices or anything else of value. whether 
tangible or intangible." 
The payment of money to Mr. Yates does not constitute a "payment received" by the 
Councilmember. Moreover, the return or refund of the Councilmember's own money is not receipt 
of income. Note, however. that were the refund of money to include money rightfully owed to 
Mr. Yates for service provided, those services will be considered a gift to the Councilmember. 
However, assuming that the refund consisted only of funds belonging to the Councilmember and 
not earned by Mr. Yates, Mr. Yates would not be a source of income to Councilmember. 


File No. A-17-124 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
By 
Assista t General Counsel, Legal Division 
JWW:jgl
